mapQuestions.question: Which part of the world is Costa Rica in?
mapQuestions.answer: Central America, the narrow strip of land between North and South America. Costa Rica sits between Nicaragua and Panama 🌎

mapQuestions.question: What is the capital of Costa Rica?
mapQuestions.answer: San José, in the central highland valley. About 1.5 million people live in the metro area, almost a third of the whole country.

mapQuestions.question: What two oceans / seas touch Costa Rica?
mapQuestions.answer: The Pacific Ocean on the west and the Caribbean Sea on the east. The country is so narrow you can drive coast to coast in 4–5 hours 🌊

mapQuestions.question: How many soldiers does Costa Rica have?
mapQuestions.answer: Zero. Costa Rica abolished its army in 1948 and has had no armed forces since. The money goes to schools and health care instead.

mapQuestions.question: How much of the world's wildlife is in Costa Rica?
mapQuestions.answer: About 5% of all the world's species live here, on only 0.03% of the world's land area. It is one of the most biodiverse places on Earth.

phrases.english: Hello / goodbye / all good / life is great

foods.name: Gallo pinto
foods.description: Rice and black beans fried together with onion, pepper and a splash of Salsa Lizano (a sweet-and-tangy national condiment). Breakfast for almost everyone in Costa Rica.

foods.name: Casado
foods.description: The classic lunch plate: rice, beans, a piece of meat (chicken, fish or beef), fried plantain, salad and a tortilla. "Casado" literally means "married", because all the elements come together.

foods.name: Patacones
foods.description: Green plantains squashed flat and fried twice until they are golden discs. Served with black beans, guacamole or salsa as a snack or side.

foods.name: Tropical fruit
foods.description: Mango, papaya, pineapple, dragon fruit, rambutan, soursop, passionfruit. Fresh and cheap from any roadside stall, with juice running down your chin.

foods.name: Costa Rican coffee
foods.description: The country produces some of the best coffee in the world, grown in the cool mountains around the central valley. Try it traditional-style, dripped through a "chorreador" cloth filter.

foods.name: Tres leches
foods.description: A sponge cake soaked in three kinds of milk (evaporated, condensed, regular) until it is impossibly wet and sweet. Eaten cold, with whipped cream on top.

scavengerItems.label: A sloth in a tree (look slowly, they barely move)
A toucan or scarlet macaw flying or perched
A monkey (white-faced capuchin, howler, or spider monkey)
A painted ox-cart, the national folk symbol of Costa Rica
A coffee plantation or coffee bushes growing on a hillside
A real volcano (Arenal, Poás, Irazú or another)
A red-eyed tree frog or any tiny brightly-coloured frog
An iguana sunning itself on a wall or in a tree
A house, shop or T-shirt printed with "Pura Vida"
A bromeliad or orchid growing on the branch of a forest tree
A bright blue morpho butterfly flashing through the jungle
A surfer riding a wave on the Pacific coast

geoMatches.description: The capital, in a high valley between mountains. Markets, museums, busy streets, and the take-off point for trips to anywhere else in the country.
A near-perfect cone in the north. Erupted lava every day from 1968 until 2010, when it went quiet. Now you can hike around it and soak in volcanic hot springs.
A high cloud forest in the mountains, where mist hangs in the trees all year and you can walk across hanging bridges through the treetops.
A small national park on the Pacific coast where rainforest meets white-sand beaches. Sloths, monkeys, iguanas and surfing in one place.
The "Costa Rican Amazon" on the Caribbean coast — a roadless network of jungle canals you can only reach by boat. Green sea turtles nest on the beach every summer.
A wild peninsula in the south-west. National Geographic called it "the most biologically intense place on Earth". Jaguars, tapirs, all four monkey species, scarlet macaws.

senses.smell: Wet rainforest after a downpour, fresh-roasted coffee, ripe pineapple and mango at a market, ocean salt mixed with jungle, woodsmoke from a hut…
senses.hear: Howler monkeys roaring through trees (like a horror movie), tropical rain on metal roofs, "Pura vida!" everywhere, parrots squawking, frogs ringing at night, surf on a black-sand beach…
senses.taste: Sweet ripe mango juice running down your chin, gallo pinto with Salsa Lizano, hot tortilla, smooth Costa Rican coffee, ice-cold guaro spirit (older), sticky tres leches cake…
senses.feel: Sticky tropical humidity, cool mist in the cloud forest, soft fine sand on a Pacific beach, hot black volcanic sand on the toes, raindrops the size of marbles…
senses.see: What is the most incredible thing right in front of you right now…

stories.question: What does "pura vida" really mean?
stories.body: Costa Ricans say "pura vida" — literally "pure life" — all day long. It is their hello, their goodbye, their thanks, their "you're welcome", their "no worries", their "life is good". Costa Rica regularly tops world lists of happiest countries, which is wild for a country with no army, no oil money, and a smaller economy than many of its neighbours. The country abolished its army in 1948, not just shrank it but ABOLISHED it, and put the money into schools and hospitals instead. Today Costa Rica has higher literacy and lower crime than many richer countries, and 99% of its electricity comes from renewable sources.

stories.question: Why are sloths so slow?
stories.body: Sloths are some of the slowest big animals on Earth. A sloth moves at about 0.2 km/h. Their muscles are about a third the size of other mammals their weight, and they have a body temperature that drops at night to save energy. They sleep 15+ hours a day. They only come down from trees about once a week to poop. Their fur grows downwards (most animals grow fur upwards) so rain runs off when they hang upside down. Algae grows in the fur, which makes them green and helps them hide in the canopy. Slow may seem useless, but it means jaguars and eagles often miss them entirely.
stories.thinkingQuestion: If a sloth needs only a tiny amount of food to live, is slowness actually a clever way to be?

stories.question: Why does Costa Rica have so many volcanoes?
stories.body: Costa Rica has more than 60 volcanoes, six of them still active. The reason: Costa Rica sits where the small Cocos tectonic plate is being pushed underneath the larger Caribbean plate, deep below the country. As the rock melts down there, it forces hot magma up to the surface as volcanoes. The Arenal volcano had a near-perfect cone and erupted lava every single day from 1968 until 2010, when it suddenly went quiet. The country also has earthquakes constantly — locals barely react to a small one, they just wait for it to pass and carry on with their coffee.

stories.question: Why did Costa Rica abolish its army?
stories.body: In 1948, after a short and bitter civil war, Costa Rica's new president José Figueres made a stunning decision: he abolished the army. Forever. He took a hammer to a wall of the old army barracks himself, as a public symbol. The money that would have gone on guns went on schools, hospitals, electricity and (later) the environment. Today Costa Rica is the only large country in the Americas with no army at all. It has been one of the most stable democracies in Latin America ever since. The decision is taught in schools as the moment the country chose a different path to its neighbours, who have had decades of military rule, civil war and instability.
stories.thinkingQuestion: If your own country chose to not spend ANY money on the military, what could be done with all that money instead?

stories.question: Why is one small forest the "most biologically intense place on Earth"?
stories.body: Corcovado, a 425 square km national park on the Osa Peninsula in the south-west of Costa Rica, has been called by National Geographic "the most biologically intense place on Earth". It has all four of Costa Rica's monkey species, jaguars, tapirs, scarlet macaws, hammerhead sharks offshore, and dozens of plant and animal species found nowhere else on Earth. Why so much life in one place? Costa Rica sits at the meeting point of North American and South American animals. It has both Pacific and Caribbean coasts. It has highland cloud forests and steamy lowland rainforests. All of those habitats are within a few hours' drive of each other, and Corcovado has the wildest, oldest patch of all of them.

convoQuestions.question: What's the best thing you have eaten in Costa Rica so far?
Have you spotted a sloth, a monkey, or a toucan yet? Which was the wildest?
Costa Ricans say "pura vida" all day long. What do you think it really means?
Volcanoes, rainforest, cloud forest, Pacific beach, Caribbean beach — favourite so far?
Have you seen anyone in a real hurry here? Or does everyone seem chilled out?
Costa Rica has no army. If we had no army at home, what could the country spend the money on?

wordSearch.hint: Costa Rican foods, places and things to spot.

animals.name: Two-toed or three-toed sloth
animals.description: In trees in Manuel Antonio, Tortuguero, anywhere with rainforest. Move very slowly.

animals.name: White-faced capuchin monkey
animals.description: Cheeky, intelligent, will absolutely steal your lunch. Common in coastal forests.

animals.name: Scarlet macaw
animals.description: Bright red-blue-yellow parrots flying in pairs above the Pacific coast (especially Carara).

animals.name: Iguana
animals.description: Green or black, sunning themselves on rocks and tree branches everywhere.

animals.name: Red-eyed tree frog
animals.description: Bright green with red eyes and orange feet. Iconic Costa Rican rainforest emblem.

animals.name: Blue morpho butterfly
animals.description: Huge iridescent blue butterfly flashing through the rainforest.
